title: It’s a shame what’s happened to radio
excerpt: Why I wouldn't enjoy returning to radio as a disk jockey.
content_html:
A repeat of an article I first published on 30 July 2009. I’ve expanded and updated it.

I signed off the air for the last time in 1994, capping a nine-year side career on the radio. People still sometimes ask me if I miss being a disk jockey, and for a long time I always wistfully answered yes. But not anymore. It’s not that I would be rusty as heck after all these years – and boy, would I. It’s that radio has changed drastically, and it just wouldn’t be any fun for me today.

I listened to a lot of radio when I was a teen. It was a companion when I was by myself doing homework or whatever. I called in requests and tried to win contests (but never did). I had a few favorite DJs, the ones who kept you listening because you wanted to know what they’d say next. The fun they were having made whatever I was doing more fun.

So when I got to college and found out about the campus radio station, WMHD, I had visions of being the kind of entertaining on-air companion I had enjoyed. I asked for and was given a weekly two-hour shift, just like every other disk jockey at the station. We could play whatever music we wanted, but my musical tastes were pretty narrow and I had trouble filling my time without always playing the same handful of artists. And I found out that wit failed me when the mic was open; I was lucky just to announce the next song without tripping over my tongue. My early shows were really pretty bad! Fortunately our puny signal covered just a few square miles, so hardly anybody heard me. Here’s a brief clip from the oldest show I have on tape, from 1986.

Needing to expand my repertoire, I had fun discovering classic and progressive rock of the ’60s and ’70s and even dabbling in heavy metal. I brought the music I found to my shift and learned how how to match key and tempo to transition smoothly between songs. I also started to find my on-air voice, as you can hear in this 1988 clip.

When I got my first part-time professional radio gig at WBOW, I had fun building and honing my on-air skills. There was a lot more to pro radio than what I’d done in college and it took time and practice to be good at it. If you’re old enough, you’ll remember the station in your town that everybody turned to for news, community information, and inoffensive music; in Terre Haute, that was WBOW. I was supposed to provide some “personality” between songs. Here’s a clip from 1992; you be the judge of whether I succeeded!

When I moved down the hall to the company’s rock station, WZZQ, I had fun connecting with listeners. I loved hearing from them when they called to make requests and play the contests. Over time, a handful of listeners came to know me on the air and called during my shifts to just say hello. I looked forward to their calls and meeting them at station events around town. It was great to know that I was providing the same kind of pleasure for them that radio gave me when I was young. It gave me the energy to do my best work, as you can hear in this clip from 1994.

After I left Terre Haute for Indianapolis I tried to get on part-time at a few stations. One polite rejection letter essentially said that I might have been fine in Terre Haute, but I wasn’t ready for the big time in Indianapolis. I decided to take the hint and went back to being just a listener, and now I’ve been out of radio more than three times as long as I was in it. In the intervening years, a number of things have changed that have made radio less fun to listen to and, I’m sure, to work in.

First, now that I’m in my 50s, advertisers don’t care about me anymore. Radio stations choose their formats to appeal to the groups that advertisers think spend the most money. Advertisers love thirtysomething moms, by the way, which is why there are so many country and adult-contemporary stations playing eleven hits in a row or forty minutes of uninterrupted music. No one radio station really reaches me.

Second, thanks to government deregulation radio is now big business. Owners have always been in it to make money, even when ownership was local or regional. But now very large corporations own so many stations and cost management seems to be more important than the quality of the on-air product. Live and local talent is all but gone, replaced by satellite-delivered formats and a form of prerecording called voicetracking. The evening jock on your favorite station probably recorded tonight’s shift this morning in a studio in Tampa or Minneapolis. Try calling the station you listen to in the evening or on the weekend. Nobody will answer, because nobody’s there. It’s cheaper that way.

Third, a change many years ago in the way radio ratings are measured has changed radio programming. As long as there have been ratings, radio stations have formatted themselves to maximize listening among the average, everyday people the ratings companies ask to track the stations they listen to. But the new way of measuring ratings, which uses a listening device called the Portable People Meter, showed a very different picture of actual listening from the older paper-diary method. It pinpointed exactly what caused listeners to change the station. This has led to stations framing programming in much shorter blocks with less human interaction with the audience. It’s why many stations have become anonymous appliances. Why listen to a station that doesn’t relate much with you when you can just listen to Spotify on shuffle instead?

I’m painting a pretty one-dimensional picture of radio’s problems; they are actually layered and complex. I don’t pretend to get all of it, but what I do get is that it has squeezed all the fun out of the business for me. There are few on-air jobs left where you can hone your craft and relate to the listeners.

When I first posted this in 2009, I called out my two favorite local on-air talents, both of whom were among my last reasons to listen to commercial radio: Steve Simpson at news/talk WIBC and Tom Berg at classic-rock WKLU. But since then WKLU was sold, changed formats to contemporary Christian, and sent poor Tom packing. Tom moved on to a station in tiny Knox, Indiana — and then he passed away after a hard battle with cancer. Steve was shifted to mornings and later fired when the station wanted to shift to a deliberate conservative bias and Steve said he didn’t know how to play along. Steve moved on to WCCO in Minneapolis and, as of 2026, has just retired.

I’ve given up. When I want to hear music, I listen on my iPhone now. When I do listen to the radio, it’s almost always to hear the news on NPR.

Meanwhile, every station I ever worked for is off the air now. The fellow who owned WBOW and WZZQ got into legal trouble that cost him his licenses. WMHD gave up its license in 2014 as student interest dwindled and airshifts couldn’t be staffed.

It’s foolish for me to assume that the institutions of my youth will endure forever. New things will come along and replace them. But at least half of why radio has become irrelevant is its own fault. And that’s a shame.

To get Down the Road in your inbox or reader six days a week, click here to subscribe!

blocks.html_en: A repeat of an article I first published on 30 July 2009. I’ve expanded and updated it.

sentences.en: A repeat of an article I first published on 30 July 2009.
sentences.zh: 重复我于 2009 年 7 月 30 日首次发表的一篇文章。

sentences.en: I’ve expanded and updated it.
sentences.zh: 我已经扩展并更新了它。

blocks.html_en: I signed off the air for the last time in 1994, capping a nine-year side career on the radio. People still sometimes ask me if I miss being a disk jockey, and for a long time I always wistfully answered yes. But not anymore. It’s not that I would be rusty as heck after all these years – and boy, would I. It’s that radio has changed drastically, and it just wouldn’t be any fun for me today.

sentences.en: I signed off the air for the last time in 1994, capping a nine-year side career on the radio.
sentences.zh: 我最后一次退出电台是在 1994 年，结束了我九年的广播生涯。

sentences.en: People still sometimes ask me if I miss being a disk jockey, and for a long time I always wistfully answered yes.
sentences.zh: 有时人们仍然会问我是否怀念当唱片骑师的时光，很长一段时间我总是若有所思地回答是的。

sentences.en: But not anymore.
sentences.zh: 但现在不再了。

sentences.en: It’s not that I would be rusty as heck after all these years – and boy, would I.
sentences.zh: 并不是说这么多年过去了我会变得生疏——天哪，我会的。

sentences.en: It’s that radio has changed drastically, and it just wouldn’t be any fun for me today.
sentences.zh: 广播已经发生了巨大的变化，今天对我来说已经没有任何乐趣了。

blocks.html_en: I listened to a lot of radio when I was a teen. It was a companion when I was by myself doing homework or whatever. I called in requests and tried to win contests (but never did). I had a few favorite DJs, the ones who kept you listening because you wanted to know what they’d say next. The fun they were having made whatever I was doing more fun.

sentences.en: I listened to a lot of radio when I was a teen.
sentences.zh: 当我十几岁的时候，我听了很多广播。

sentences.en: It was a companion when I was by myself doing homework or whatever.
sentences.zh: 当我独自做作业或做其他事情时，它是我的伴侣。

sentences.en: I called in requests and tried to win contests (but never did).
sentences.zh: 我提出请求并试图赢得比赛（但从未成功）。

sentences.en: I had a few favorite DJs, the ones who kept you listening because you wanted to know what they’d say next.
sentences.zh: 我有一些最喜欢的 DJ，他们让你一直听着，因为你想知道他们接下来会说什么。

sentences.en: The fun they were having made whatever I was doing more fun.
sentences.zh: 他们所带来的乐趣让我所做的一切变得更加有趣。

blocks.html_en: So when I got to college and found out about the campus radio station, WMHD, I had visions of being the kind of entertaining on-air companion I had enjoyed. I asked for and was given a weekly two-hour shift, just like every other disk jockey at the station. We could play whatever music we wanted, but my musical tastes were pretty narrow and I had trouble filling my time without always playing the same handful of artists. And I found out that wit failed me when the mic was open; I was lucky just to announce the next song without tripping over my tongue. My early shows were really pretty bad! Fortunately our puny signal covered just a few square miles, so hardly anybody heard me. Here’s a brief clip from the oldest show I have on tape, from 1986.

sentences.en: So when I got to college and found out about the campus radio station, WMHD, I had visions of being the kind of entertaining on-air companion I had enjoyed.
sentences.zh: 因此，当我进入大学并发现校园广播电台 WMHD 时，我就梦想成为我喜欢的那种有趣的广播伴侣。

sentences.en: I asked for and was given a weekly two-hour shift, just like every other disk jockey at the station.
sentences.zh: 我要求并得到了每周两小时的轮班，就像电台的其他唱片骑师一样。

sentences.en: We could play whatever music we wanted, but my musical tastes were pretty narrow and I had trouble filling my time without always playing the same handful of artists.
sentences.zh: 我们可以演奏任何我们想要的音乐，但我的音乐品味非常狭窄，如果不总是演奏同一群艺术家，我很难打发时间。

sentences.en: And I found out that wit failed me when the mic was open; I was lucky just to announce the next song without tripping over my tongue.
sentences.zh: 我发现当麦克风打开时我就失去了理智；我很幸运，只是宣布了下一首歌曲，而没有被我的舌头绊倒。

sentences.en: My early shows were really pretty bad!
sentences.zh: 我早期的表演真的很糟糕！

sentences.en: Fortunately our puny signal covered just a few square miles, so hardly anybody heard me.
sentences.zh: 幸运的是，我们微弱的信号只覆盖了几平方英里，所以几乎没有人听到我的声音。

sentences.en: Here’s a brief clip from the oldest show I have on tape, from 1986.
sentences.zh: 这是我录制的最古老的节目的一个简短片段，来自 1986 年。

blocks.html_en: Needing to expand my repertoire, I had fun discovering classic and progressive rock of the ’60s and ’70s and even dabbling in heavy metal. I brought the music I found to my shift and learned how how to match key and tempo to transition smoothly between songs. I also started to find my on-air voice, as you can hear in this 1988 clip.

sentences.en: Needing to expand my repertoire, I had fun discovering classic and progressive rock of the ’60s and ’70s and even dabbling in heavy metal.
sentences.zh: 由于需要扩展我的演奏曲目，我乐于发现 60 年代和 70 年代的经典和前卫摇滚，甚至涉足重金属。

sentences.en: I brought the music I found to my shift and learned how how to match key and tempo to transition smoothly between songs.
sentences.zh: 我把我找到的音乐带到了我的轮班中，并学习了如何匹配调性和节奏以在歌曲之间顺利过渡。

sentences.en: I also started to find my on-air voice, as you can hear in this 1988 clip.
sentences.zh: 我也开始寻找我的广播声音，正如您在 1988 年的剪辑中听到的那样。

There was a lot more to pro radio than what I’d done in college and it took time and practice to be good at it. I was supposed to provide some “personality” between songs. Here’s a clip from 1992; you be the judge of whether I succeeded!

sentences.en: When I got my first part-time professional radio gig at WBOW, I had fun building and honing my on-air skills.
sentences.zh: 当我在 WBOW 获得第一份兼职专业广播演出时，我很享受建立和磨练我的广播技能的乐趣。

sentences.en: There was a lot more to pro radio than what I’d done in college and it took time and practice to be good at it.
sentences.zh: 职业无线电比我在大学所做的要多得多，需要时间和练习才能擅长。

sentences.en: If you’re old enough, you’ll remember the station in your town that everybody turned to for news, community information, and inoffensive music; in Terre Haute, that was WBOW.
sentences.zh: 如果您足够大，您会记得镇上每个人都去那里获取新闻、社区信息和无害音乐的电台；在特雷霍特，那就是 WBOW。

sentences.en: I was supposed to provide some “personality” between songs.
sentences.zh: 我应该在歌曲之间提供一些“个性”。

sentences.en: Here’s a clip from 1992; you be the judge of whether I succeeded!
sentences.zh: 这是 1992 年的剪辑；你来评判我是否成功！

blocks.html_en: When I moved down the hall to the company’s rock station, WZZQ, I had fun connecting with listeners. I loved hearing from them when they called to make requests and play the contests. Over time, a handful of listeners came to know me on the air and called during my shifts to just say hello. I looked forward to their calls and meeting them at station events around town. It was great to know that I was providing the same kind of pleasure for them that radio gave me when I was young. It gave me the energy to do my best work, as you can hear in this clip from 1994.

sentences.en: When I moved down the hall to the company’s rock station, WZZQ, I had fun connecting with listeners.
sentences.zh: 当我沿着大厅走到公司的摇滚电台 WZZQ 时，我很高兴与听众交流。

sentences.en: I loved hearing from them when they called to make requests and play the contests.
sentences.zh: 当他们打电话提出要求并参加比赛时，我很高兴收到他们的来信。

sentences.en: Over time, a handful of listeners came to know me on the air and called during my shifts to just say hello.
sentences.zh: 随着时间的推移，一些听众在广播中认识了我，并在我轮班期间打电话来打招呼。

sentences.en: I looked forward to their calls and meeting them at station events around town.
sentences.zh: 我期待着他们的电话并在城镇周围的车站活动中与他们见面。

sentences.en: It was great to know that I was providing the same kind of pleasure for them that radio gave me when I was young.
sentences.zh: 很高兴知道我正在为他们提供与我年轻时收音机给我带来的同样的快乐。

sentences.en: It gave me the energy to do my best work, as you can hear in this clip from 1994.
sentences.zh: 正如您在 1994 年的这段视频中所听到的那样，它给了我全力以赴地完成工作的能量。

blocks.html_en: After I left Terre Haute for Indianapolis I tried to get on part-time at a few stations. sentences.zh: 离开特雷霍特前往印第安纳波利斯后，我尝试在几个车站做兼职。

sentences.en: One polite rejection letter essentially said that I might have been fine in Terre Haute, but I wasn’t ready for the big time in Indianapolis.
sentences.zh: 一封礼貌的拒绝信基本上是在说，我在特雷霍特可能过得很好，但我还没有准备好在印第安纳波利斯度过美好时光。

sentences.en: I decided to take the hint and went back to being just a listener, and now I’ve been out of radio more than three times as long as I was in it.
sentences.zh: 我决定接受这个暗示，重新做一个听众，现在我离开广播的时间是我在广播中的时间的三倍多。

sentences.en: In the intervening years, a number of things have changed that have made radio less fun to listen to and, I’m sure, to work in.
sentences.zh: 在接下来的几年里，许多事情发生了变化，使得收听广播变得不再那么有趣，而且我确信，工作也变得不再那么有趣。

Advertisers love thirtysomething moms, by the way, which is why there are so many country and adult-contemporary stations playing eleven hits in a row or forty minutes of uninterrupted music. sentences.zh: 首先，现在我已经50多岁了，广告商不再关心我了。

sentences.zh: 广播电台选择其格式来吸引广告商认为花钱最多的群体。

sentences.en: Advertisers love thirtysomething moms, by the way, which is why there are so many country and adult-contemporary stations playing eleven hits in a row or forty minutes of uninterrupted music.
sentences.zh: 顺便说一句，广告商喜欢三十多岁的妈妈，这就是为什么有这么多乡村和成人当代电台连续播放十一首热门歌曲或四十分钟不间断的音乐。

sentences.en: No one radio station really reaches me.
sentences.zh: 没有一家广播电台真正能传达到我的心声。

sentences.en: Second, thanks to government deregulation radio is now big business.
sentences.zh: 其次，由于政府放松管制，广播现在已成为一项大生意。

sentences.en: Owners have always been in it to make money, even when ownership was local or regional.
sentences.zh: 业主一直都是为了赚钱，即使所有权是本地或区域性的。

sentences.en: But now very large corporations own so many stations and cost management seems to be more important than the quality of the on-air product.
sentences.zh: 但现在非常大的公司拥有如此多的电台，成本管理似乎比播出产品的质量更重要。

sentences.en: Live and local talent is all but gone, replaced by satellite-delivered formats and a form of prerecording called voicetracking.
sentences.zh: 现场和本地人才几乎消失了，取而代之的是卫星传输的格式和一种称为语音跟踪的预录音形式。

sentences.en: The evening jock on your favorite station probably recorded tonight’s shift this morning in a studio in Tampa or Minneapolis.
sentences.zh: 你最喜欢的电台的晚间节目主持人可能今天早上在坦帕或明尼阿波利斯的录音室录制了今晚的节目。

sentences.en: Try calling the station you listen to in the evening or on the weekend.
sentences.zh: 尝试致电您在晚上或周末收听的电台。

sentences.en: Nobody will answer, because nobody’s there.
sentences.zh: 没有人会回答，因为没有人在那里。

sentences.en: It’s cheaper that way.
sentences.zh: 这样比较便宜。

blocks.html_en: Third, a change many years ago in the way radio ratings are measured has changed radio programming. As long as there have been ratings, radio stations have formatted themselves to maximize listening among the average, everyday people the ratings companies ask to track the stations they listen to. But the new way of measuring ratings, which uses a listening device called the Portable People Meter, showed a very different picture of actual listening from the older paper-diary method. It pinpointed exactly what caused listeners to change the station. This has led to stations framing programming in much shorter blocks with less human interaction with the audience. It’s why many stations have become anonymous appliances. Why listen to a station that doesn’t relate much with you when you can just listen to Spotify on shuffle instead?

sentences.en: Third, a change many years ago in the way radio ratings are measured has changed radio programming.
sentences.zh: 第三，多年前广播收视率测量方式的变化已经改变了广播节目。

sentences.en: As long as there have been ratings, radio stations have formatted themselves to maximize listening among the average, everyday people the ratings companies ask to track the stations they listen to.
sentences.zh: 只要有收视率，广播电台就会自我调整，以最大限度地提高普通民众的收听率，收视率公司要求跟踪他们收听的电台。

sentences.en: But the new way of measuring ratings, which uses a listening device called the Portable People Meter, showed a very different picture of actual listening from the older paper-diary method.
sentences.zh: 但测量收视率的新方法使用了一种名为“便携式人员计”的收听设备，它显示出与旧的纸质日记方法截然不同的实际收听情况。

sentences.en: It pinpointed exactly what caused listeners to change the station.
sentences.zh: 它准确地指出了导致听众更换电台的原因。

sentences.en: This has led to stations framing programming in much shorter blocks with less human interaction with the audience.
sentences.zh: 这导致电视台将节目编排成更短的区块，与观众的互动更少。

sentences.en: It’s why many stations have become anonymous appliances.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么许多电台已成为匿名设备的原因。

sentences.en: Why listen to a station that doesn’t relate much with you when you can just listen to Spotify on shuffle instead?
sentences.zh: 当您可以通过随机播放 Spotify 来收听时，为什么还要收听与您关系不大的电台呢？

blocks.html_en: I’m painting a pretty one-dimensional picture of radio’s problems; they are actually layered and complex. I don’t pretend to get all of it, but what I do get is that it has squeezed all the fun out of the business for me. There are few on-air jobs left where you can hone your craft and relate to the listeners.

sentences.en: I’m painting a pretty one-dimensional picture of radio’s problems; they are actually layered and complex.
sentences.zh: 我正在描绘一幅无线电问题的一维图景；它们实际上是分层且复杂的。

sentences.en: I don’t pretend to get all of it, but what I do get is that it has squeezed all the fun out of the business for me.
sentences.zh: 我并不假装得到了这一切，但我确实得到的是，它已经榨干了我从这个行业中得到的所有乐趣。

sentences.en: There are few on-air jobs left where you can hone your craft and relate to the listeners.
sentences.zh: 几乎没有什么直播工作可以让你磨练自己的技艺并与听众建立联系。

blocks.html_en: When I first posted this in 2009, I called out my two favorite local on-air talents, both of whom were among my last reasons to listen to commercial radio: Steve Simpson at news/talk WIBC and Tom Berg at classic-rock WKLU. But since then WKLU was sold, changed formats to contemporary Christian, and sent poor Tom packing. Tom moved on to a station in tiny Knox, Indiana — and then he passed away after a hard battle with cancer. Steve was shifted to mornings and later fired when the station wanted to shift to a deliberate conservative bias and Steve said he didn’t know how to play along. Steve moved on to WCCO in Minneapolis and, as of 2026, has just retired.

sentences.en: When I first posted this in 2009, I called out my two favorite local on-air talents, both of whom were among my last reasons to listen to commercial radio: Steve Simpson at news/talk WIBC and Tom Berg at classic-rock WKLU.
sentences.zh: 当我在 2009 年第一次发布这篇文章时，我点出了我最喜欢的两位本地广播人才，他们都是我最后听商业广播的原因之一：新闻/谈话 WIBC 的史蒂夫·辛普森 (Steve Simpson) 和经典摇滚 WKLU 的汤姆·伯格 (Tom Berg)。

sentences.en: But since then WKLU was sold, changed formats to contemporary Christian, and sent poor Tom packing.
sentences.zh: 但从那时起，WKLU 被出售，将格式改为当代基督教，并把可怜的汤姆打发走了。

sentences.en: Tom moved on to a station in tiny Knox, Indiana — and then he passed away after a hard battle with cancer.
sentences.zh: 汤姆搬到了印第安纳州诺克斯小镇的一个车站，然后在与癌症的艰苦斗争后去世了。

sentences.en: Steve was shifted to mornings and later fired when the station wanted to shift to a deliberate conservative bias and Steve said he didn’t know how to play along.
sentences.zh: 史蒂夫被调到早上，后来当电视台想要转向故意的保守派偏见时被解雇，史蒂夫说他不知道如何配合。

sentences.en: Steve moved on to WCCO in Minneapolis and, as of 2026, has just retired.
sentences.zh: Steve 转到明尼阿波利斯的 WCCO，并于 2026 年刚刚退休。

blocks.html_en: I’ve given up. When I want to hear music, I listen on my iPhone now. When I do listen to the radio, it’s almost always to hear the news on NPR.

sentences.en: I’ve given up.
sentences.zh: 我已经放弃了。

sentences.en: When I want to hear music, I listen on my iPhone now.
sentences.zh: 当我想听音乐时，我现在就用 iPhone 听。

sentences.en: When I do listen to the radio, it’s almost always to hear the news on NPR.
sentences.zh: 当我听广播时，几乎总是听到 NPR 上的新闻。

blocks.html_en: Meanwhile, every station I ever worked for is off the air now. The fellow who owned WBOW and WZZQ got into legal trouble that cost him his licenses. WMHD gave up its license in 2014 as student interest dwindled and airshifts couldn’t be staffed.

sentences.en: Meanwhile, every station I ever worked for is off the air now.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，我曾经工作过的每个电台现在都停播了。

sentences.en: The fellow who owned WBOW and WZZQ got into legal trouble that cost him his licenses.
sentences.zh: 拥有 WBOW 和 WZZQ 的家伙陷入了法律麻烦，导致他失去了执照。

sentences.en: WMHD gave up its license in 2014 as student interest dwindled and airshifts couldn’t be staffed.
sentences.zh: 由于学生兴趣下降且无法配备空勤人员，WMHD 于 2014 年放弃了执照。

blocks.html_en: It’s foolish for me to assume that the institutions of my youth will endure forever. New things will come along and replace them. But at least half of why radio has become irrelevant is its own fault. And that’s a shame.

sentences.en: It’s foolish for me to assume that the institutions of my youth will endure forever.
sentences.zh: 我认为我年轻时的机构会永远存在，这是愚蠢的。

sentences.en: New things will come along and replace them.
sentences.zh: 新事物将会出现并取代它们。

sentences.en: But at least half of why radio has become irrelevant is its own fault.
sentences.zh: 但广播之所以变得无关紧要，至少有一半是它自己的错。

sentences.en: And that’s a shame.
sentences.zh: 这太遗憾了。

blocks.html_en: To get Down the Road in your inbox or reader six days a week, click here to subscribe!

sentences.en: To get Down the Road in your inbox or reader six days a week, click here to subscribe!
sentences.zh: 要每周六天在您的收件箱或读者中获取信息，请单击此处订阅！

content_html_zh:
重复我于 2009 年 7 月 30 日首次发表的一篇文章。 我已经扩展并更新了它。

我最后一次退出电台是在 1994 年，结束了我九年的广播生涯。 有时人们仍然会问我是否怀念当唱片骑师的时光，很长一段时间我总是若有所思地回答是的。 但现在不再了。 并不是说这么多年过去了我会变得生疏——天哪，我会的。 广播已经发生了巨大的变化，今天对我来说已经没有任何乐趣了。

当我十几岁的时候，我听了很多广播。 当我独自做作业或做其他事情时，它是我的伴侣。 我提出请求并试图赢得比赛（但从未成功）。 我有一些最喜欢的 DJ，他们让你一直听着，因为你想知道他们接下来会说什么。 他们所带来的乐趣让我所做的一切变得更加有趣。

因此，当我进入大学并发现校园广播电台 WMHD 时，我就梦想成为我喜欢的那种有趣的广播伴侣。 我要求并得到了每周两小时的轮班，就像电台的其他唱片骑师一样。 我们可以演奏任何我们想要的音乐，但我的音乐品味非常狭窄，如果不总是演奏同一群艺术家，我很难打发时间。 我发现当麦克风打开时我就失去了理智；我很幸运，只是宣布了下一首歌曲，而没有被我的舌头绊倒。 我早期的表演真的很糟糕！ 幸运的是，我们微弱的信号只覆盖了几平方英里，所以几乎没有人听到我的声音。 这是我录制的最古老的节目的一个简短片段，来自 1986 年。

由于需要扩展我的演奏曲目，我乐于发现 60 年代和 70 年代的经典和前卫摇滚，甚至涉足重金属。 我把我找到的音乐带到了我的轮班中，并学习了如何匹配调性和节奏以在歌曲之间顺利过渡。 我也开始寻找我的广播声音，正如您在 1988 年的剪辑中听到的那样。

当我在 WBOW 获得第一份兼职专业广播演出时，我很享受建立和磨练我的广播技能的乐趣。 职业无线电比我在大学所做的要多得多，需要时间和练习才能擅长。 如果您足够大，您会记得镇上每个人都去那里获取新闻、社区信息和无害音乐的电台；在特雷霍特，那就是 WBOW。 我应该在歌曲之间提供一些“个性”。 这是 1992 年的剪辑；你来评判我是否成功！

当我沿着大厅走到公司的摇滚电台 WZZQ 时，我很高兴与听众交流。 当他们打电话提出要求并参加比赛时，我很高兴收到他们的来信。 随着时间的推移，一些听众在广播中认识了我，并在我轮班期间打电话来打招呼。 我期待着他们的电话并在城镇周围的车站活动中与他们见面。 很高兴知道我正在为他们提供与我年轻时收音机给我带来的同样的快乐。 正如您在 1994 年的这段视频中所听到的那样，它给了我全力以赴地完成工作的能量。

离开特雷霍特前往印第安纳波利斯后，我尝试在几个车站做兼职。 一封礼貌的拒绝信基本上是在说，我在特雷霍特可能过得很好，但我还没有准备好在印第安纳波利斯度过美好时光。 我决定接受这个暗示，重新做一个听众，现在我离开广播的时间是我在广播中的时间的三倍多。 在接下来的几年里，许多事情发生了变化，使得收听广播变得不再那么有趣，而且我确信，工作也变得不再那么有趣。

首先，现在我已经50多岁了，广告商不再关心我了。 广播电台选择其格式来吸引广告商认为花钱最多的群体。 顺便说一句，广告商喜欢三十多岁的妈妈，这就是为什么有这么多乡村和成人当代电台连续播放十一首热门歌曲或四十分钟不间断的音乐。 没有一家广播电台真正能传达到我的心声。

其次，由于政府放松管制，广播现在已成为一项大生意。 业主一直都是为了赚钱，即使所有权是本地或区域性的。 但现在非常大的公司拥有如此多的电台，成本管理似乎比播出产品的质量更重要。 现场和本地人才几乎消失了，取而代之的是卫星传输的格式和一种称为语音跟踪的预录音形式。 你最喜欢的电台的晚间节目主持人可能今天早上在坦帕或明尼阿波利斯的录音室录制了今晚的节目。 尝试致电您在晚上或周末收听的电台。 没有人会回答，因为没有人在那里。 这样比较便宜。

第三，多年前广播收视率测量方式的变化已经改变了广播节目。 只要有收视率，广播电台就会自我调整，以最大限度地提高普通民众的收听率，收视率公司要求跟踪他们收听的电台。 但测量收视率的新方法使用了一种名为“便携式人员计”的收听设备，它显示出与旧的纸质日记方法截然不同的实际收听情况。 它准确地指出了导致听众更换电台的原因。 这导致电视台将节目编排成更短的区块，与观众的互动更少。 这就是为什么许多电台已成为匿名设备的原因。 当您可以通过随机播放 Spotify 来收听时，为什么还要收听与您关系不大的电台呢？

我正在描绘一幅无线电问题的一维图景；它们实际上是分层且复杂的。 我并不假装得到了这一切，但我确实得到的是，它已经榨干了我从这个行业中得到的所有乐趣。 几乎没有什么直播工作可以让你磨练自己的技艺并与听众建立联系。

当我在 2009 年第一次发布这篇文章时，我点出了我最喜欢的两位本地广播人才，他们都是我最后听商业广播的原因之一：新闻/谈话 WIBC 的史蒂夫·辛普森 (Steve Simpson) 和经典摇滚 WKLU 的汤姆·伯格 (Tom Berg)。 但从那时起，WKLU 被出售，将格式改为当代基督教，并把可怜的汤姆打发走了。 汤姆搬到了印第安纳州诺克斯小镇的一个车站，然后在与癌症的艰苦斗争后去世了。 史蒂夫被调到早上，后来当电视台想要转向故意的保守派偏见时被解雇，史蒂夫说他不知道如何配合。 Steve 转到明尼阿波利斯的 WCCO，并于 2026 年刚刚退休。

我已经放弃了。 当我想听音乐时，我现在就用 iPhone 听。 当我听广播时，几乎总是听到 NPR 上的新闻。

与此同时，我曾经工作过的每个电台现在都停播了。 拥有 WBOW 和 WZZQ 的家伙陷入了法律麻烦，导致他失去了执照。 由于学生兴趣下降且无法配备空勤人员，WMHD 于 2014 年放弃了执照。

我认为我年轻时的机构会永远存在，这是愚蠢的。 新事物将会出现并取代它们。 但广播之所以变得无关紧要，至少有一半是它自己的错。 这太遗憾了。
